coredata.dc:title: Phase equilibria of the Fe–Nb–Ti system at 900°C
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2004 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Honghui
Du, Yong
Yuan, Zhaohui
Li, Shitong
Schuster, J.C.
He, Yuehui
coredata.dc:description: The isothermal section at 900°C of the Fe–Nb–Ti system was determined using the diffusion couple approach with the aid of equilibrated alloys. The specimens were examined by means of optical microscopy, scanning electron microscopy, and electron probe microanalysis. No ternary compound was found. The isothermal section consists of five single-phase regions, six two-phase regions and two three-phase regions. The solubility of Nb in TiFe were determined to be 6at.%, and the solubility of Ti in μ-Nb19Fe21 to be 23.6at.%. NbFe2 and TiFe2 form a continuous solid solution (Nb, Ti)Fe2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe–Nb–Ti ternary system
Diffusion couple
Phase equilibrium
Isothermal section

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Backscattered electron image (BSE) of a Fe–Nb–Ti couple annealed at 900°C for 432h.

Backscattered electron images (BSE) of the NbTi alloy/Fe diffusion couples annealed at 900°C for 260h. (a) Diffusion couple Nb20Ti80/Fe; (b) diffusion couple Nb35Ti65/Fe; (c) diffusion couple Nb50Ti50/Fe; (d) diffusion couple Nb65Ti35/Fe.

Backscattered electron images (BSE) of three of the ternary alloys annealed at 900°C for 600h. (a) Alloy Fe55.0Nb13.0Ti32.0 (in at.%); (b) alloy Fe44.0Nb18.0Ti38.0 (in at.%); (c) alloy Fe30.0Nb12.0Ti58.0 (in at.%).
Isothermal section at 900°C of the Fe–Nb–Ti system.
XRD patterns of the alloys 2, 4 and 5, annealed at 900°C for 600h. (a) Alloy 2 Fe44.0Nb18.0Ti38.0 (in at.%); (b) alloy 4 Fe38.0Nb36.0Ti26.0 (in at.%); (c) alloy 5 Fe38.0Nb57.0Ti5.0 (in at.%).
The nominal compositions of five Nb–Ti binary alloys used to prepare the NbTi alloy/Fe diffusion couples (in at.%)
The nominal compositions of five Fe–Nb–Ti ternary alloys (in at.%)
The tie-line data on the phase equilibria of the Fe–Nb–Ti system at 900°C (in at.%)
The tie-triangle data on the phase equilibria of the Fe–Nb–Ti system at 900°C (in at.%)

article.ce:title: Phase equilibria of the Fe–Nb–Ti system at 900°C

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410083, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan, Shandong 250061, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physical Chemistry, University of Vienna, A-1090 Wien, Austria

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The isothermal section at 900°C of the Fe–Nb–Ti system was determined using the diffusion couple approach with the aid of equilibrated alloys. The specimens were examined by means of optical microscopy, scanning electron microscopy, and electron probe microanalysis. No ternary compound was found. The isothermal section consists of five single-phase regions, six two-phase regions and two three-phase regions. The solubility of Nb in TiFe were determined to be 6at.%, and the solubility of Ti in μ-Nb19Fe21 to be 23.6at.%. NbFe2 and TiFe2 form a continuous solid solution (Nb, Ti)Fe2.

ce:section.ce:para: The Fe–Nb–Ti system is attractive because it is the subsystem of the Fe–Nb–Ti–C–N steel system [1] and the cemented carbides system [2]. Raghavan [3] recently evaluated the Fe–Nb–Ti system. Up to now, very limited information on the phase equilibria of the ternary system is available. Shurin et al. [4] investigated the phase equilibria in the Ti-rich corner of the Fe–Nb–Ti system at 750 and 600°C, and suggested that a ternary compound τ of unknown structure with the approximate formula Fe3NbTi6 was observed. The present study is intended to investigate the phase equilibria of the Fe–Nb–Ti system at 900°C.

The Fe–Nb system was thermodynamically assessed by Toffolon and Servant [5]. There are two intermediate phases in the Fe–Nb system, i.e., NbFe2 and μ. The μ phase was designated as Nb19Fe21 by Zelaya et al. [6]. There are two intermediate phases in the Fe–Ti system, namely TiFe2 and TiFe. Jonsson [7] thermodynamically evaluated the Fe–Ti system. The Nb–Ti phase diagram [8] is a simple system without any intermetallic compound.

Iron (99.5wt.%), niobium (99.8wt.%) and titanium (99.9wt.%) were used as the starting materials. The binary Nb–Ti couples were first prepared by diffusion welding the elemental bars in a silica capsule back-filled with high purity argon at 1100°C for 72h. One of the Nb–Ti couples and a Fe block were ground, polished, cleaned, and then swathed together with Mo wires to make a Fe–Nb–Ti diffusion couple, which was wrapped in Mo foil. Three Fe–Nb–Ti diffusion couples, which were sealed in a silica capsule back-filled with high purity argon, were annealed in an L4514-type diffusion furnace at 900±1°C (Qingdao Instrument & Equipment Co. Ltd., China) for 432h and subsequently quenched in water. In order to prepare the NbTi alloy/Fe diffusion couples, five Nb–Ti binary alloys listed in Table 1 were prepared by arc melting pure elements in an arc furnace under argon atmosphere using a non-consumable tungsten electrode. The ingots were remelted three times to improve their homogeneity. From the ingots slices of approximate dimensions of 4mm×4mm×12mm were cut. The NbTi alloy/Fe diffusion couples, which were prepared with the same method as above-mentioned, were annealed at 900±1°C for 260h and quenched in water.

After standard metallographic preparation, all of the Fe–Nb–Ti couples were examined first using optical microscopy (Leica DMLP, Germany) and then using SEM. The phase equilibria in the couples were determined by EPMA (JXA-8800R, JEOL, Japan). Subsequently, five Fe–Nb–Ti ternary alloys listed in Table 2 were selected on the basis of the experimental results obtained with EPMA from the Fe–Nb–Ti diffusion couples. The specimens were annealed in an L4514-type diffusion furnace at 900±1°C for 600h and quenched in water. The metallographic samples were first examined using optical microscopy, and then analyzed using EPMA.

The microstructure of a Fe–Nb–Ti couple annealed at 900°C for 432h is shown in Fig. 1 . The microstructures of the NbTi alloy/Fe diffusion couples annealed at 900°C for 260h are presented in Fig. 2 . The backscattered electron images (BSE) of three of the ternary alloys annealed at 900°C for 600h are given in Fig. 3 .

The tie-line data and the tie-triangle data obtained with EPMA from the Fe–Nb–Ti diffusion couples and equilibrated alloys are listed in Tables 3 and 4
, respectively. Using these experimental data, the isothermal section at 900°C was constructed in Fig. 4 .

The data close to the boundary binary systems were in accord with the literature data [6,7]. Our experimental results showed that NbFe2 and TiFe2 form a continuous solid solution, namely (Nb, Ti)Fe2. At 900°C, the solubility of Nb in TiFe were determined to be 6at.% and the solubility of Ti in μ to be 23.6at.%. High solubility of Fe in (Ti, Nb) was observed up to Nb-rich corner. Two three-phase equilibria (Nb, Ti)Fe2 +μ+TiFe and μ+TiFe+(Nb, Ti), were accurately determined.

As shown in Fig. 2, the diffusion paths in the diffusion couples Nb20Ti80/Fe and Nb35Ti65/Fe were the same, i.e., (Nb, Ti)→TiFe→(Nb, Ti)Fe2 →(Fe). There is a three-phase conjunction interface in the NbTi side of the diffusion couple Nb50Ti50/Fe, as can be seen in Fig. 2(c). For the diffusion couples Nb65Ti35/Fe and Nb80Ti20/Fe, the diffusion paths were (Nb, Ti)→μ→(Nb, Ti)Fe2 →(Fe).

Shurin et al. [4] claimed that a ternary compound τ with the approximate formula Fe3NbTi6 was observed at 750 and 600°C. The alloy 3 listed in Table 2 was designed to see if the ternary compound τ occur at 900°C. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the equilibrated alloy 3 shows the existence of the two phases TiFe and (Nb, Ti), the equilibrium compositions of which were determined to be Fe48.8Nb1.1Ti50.1 and Fe11.7Nb21.9Ti66.4 (in at.%), respectively. It was demonstrated that the ternary compound τ, which was found at 750 and 600°C by Shurin et al. [4], was not observed at 900°C. Fig. 5 (a–c) shows the XRD patterns corresponding to the alloys 2, 4 and 5 listed in Table 2, respectively. Both the alloys 2 and 4 were characterized as three phases, which were μ, TiFe, and (Nb, Ti). The alloy 5 was characterized as two phases, which were μ and (Nb, Ti).
The isothermal section at 900°C of the Fe–Nb–Ti system was determined using the diffusion couple approach with the aid of equilibrated alloys.

No ternary compound was found. At 900°C, the solubility of Nb in TiFe were determined to be 6.0at.%, and the solubility of Ti in μ to be 23.6at.%. NbFe2 and TiFe2 form a continuous solid solution (Nb, Ti)Fe2.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 50323008) is greatly acknowledged. One of the authors (Yong Du) acknowledges Furong Chair Professorship Program released by Hunan Province of PR China for financial support. The donation of Leica DMLP microscope from Alexander von Humboldt Foundation is greatly appreciated.
